From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Group of wagons traveling together

This article is about the group of animal-powered vehicles traveling together. For other uses, see [Wagon train (disambiguation)](/wiki/Wagon_train_(disambiguation) "Wagon train (disambiguation)").

"Baggage train" redirects here. For the military use of the term, see [Train (military)](/wiki/Train_(military) "Train (military)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(May 2012)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A **wagon train** is a group of wagons traveling together. Before the extensive use of [military vehicles](/wiki/Military_vehicle "Military vehicle"), **baggage trains** followed an army with supplies and ammunition.

In the [American West](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier"), settlers traveling across the plains and mountain passes in [covered wagons](/wiki/Covered_wagon "Covered wagon") banded together for mutual assistance. Although wagon trains are associated with the [Old West](/wiki/Old_West "Old West"), the [Trekboers](/wiki/Trekboer "Trekboer") of South Africa also traveled in caravans of covered wagons.

## In migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wagon_train&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: In migration")]

### Transit, traces, and trails

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wagon_train&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Transit, traces, and trails")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Pioneers_Crossing_the_Plains_of_Nebraska_by_C.C.A._Christensen.png/250px-Pioneers_Crossing_the_Plains_of_Nebraska_by_C.C.A._Christensen.png)](/wiki/File:Pioneers_Crossing_the_Plains_of_Nebraska_by_C.C.A._Christensen.png)

Pioneers crossing the plains of Nebraska

Wagon trains followed several trails in the [American West](/wiki/American_frontier "American frontier"), nearly all originating at [Independence, Missouri](/wiki/Independence,_Missouri "Independence, Missouri").[[1]](#cite_note-smith-1) Perhaps the most famous wagon train trail was the [Oregon Trail](/wiki/Oregon_Trail "Oregon Trail") which had a span of over 2,000 mi (3,200 km).[[2]](#cite_note-R1-2) Other paths included the [Santa Fe Trail](/wiki/Santa_Fe_Trail "Santa Fe Trail"), the [Chisholm Trail](/wiki/Chisholm_Trail "Chisholm Trail"), the [California Trail](/wiki/California_Trail "California Trail") (which split southwestward from the Oregon Trail), the [Mormon Trail](/wiki/Mormon_Trail "Mormon Trail"), and the [Old Spanish Trail](/wiki/Old_Spanish_Trail_(trade_route) "Old Spanish Trail (trade route)").

Although "wagon train" suggests a line of wagons, when terrain permitted, wagons would often fan out and travel abreast to minimize the amount of dust blown onto other wagons. Travel by wagon train occurred primarily between the 1840s–1880s, diminishing after completion of the [first transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad"). Some remnants of wagon ruts along the well-traveled trails are still visible today.[[1]](#cite_note-smith-1)

### Organization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wagon_train&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Organization")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/WagonTrn.jpg/250px-WagonTrn.jpg)](/wiki/File:WagonTrn.jpg)

1912 [Pioneer Day](/wiki/Pioneer_Day_(Utah) "Pioneer Day (Utah)") re-enactment of a wagon train in [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah")

Westward movement originally began in small groups, but well-funded travelers with 100 or more wagons could employ professional [wagon masters](/wiki/Wagon_master "Wagon master") (or trail masters) and [hostlers](/wiki/Hostler "Hostler").

Overland emigrants discovered smaller groups of 20 to 40 wagons were more manageable than larger ones, especially without professional wagon masters. Many operated under democratic principles, creating bylaws and electing a captain. In reality, a captain had limited authority. His role was largely confined to getting everyone moving in the morning and selecting when and where to camp at night.[[3]](#cite_note-OCTA-3)

Membership of wagon trains was generally fluid and wagons frequently joined or left trains depending on the needs and wishes of their owners. An accident or illness, for instance, may force someone to fall behind and wait for the next train, or an emigrant might "whip up" to overtake a forward train after a quarrel. Some might break away to settle in [Colorado Territory](/wiki/Colorado_Territory "Colorado Territory") or other territories along the way.

At night, wagon trains were often formed into a circle or square for shelter from wind or weather, and to corral the emigrants' animals in the center to prevent them from running away or being stolen by [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"). While Native Americans might attempt to raid horses under the cover of darkness, they rarely attacked a train. Contrary to popular belief, wagons were rarely circled defensively.[[4]](#cite_note-R2-4)

### Modern-day treks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wagon_train&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Modern-day treks")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/%22Indian_teams_hauling_60_miles_to_market_the_1100_bushels_of_wheat_raised_by_the_school._It_brought_four_cents_more_than_-_NARA_-_519190.tif/lossy-page1-448px-%22Indian_teams_hauling_60_miles_to_market_the_1100_bushels_of_wheat_raised_by_the_school._It_brought_four_cents_more_than_-_NARA_-_519190.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Indian_teams_hauling_60_miles_to_market_the_1100_bushels_of_wheat_raised_by_the_school._It_brought_four_cents_more_than_-_NARA_-_519190.tif)

Native American teams hauling 60 miles (97 km) to market the 1,100 bushels of wheat raised by the school at Seger Colony,[[5]](#cite_note-5) Oklahoma Territory, c. 1900

Today, covered wagon trains are used to give an authentic experience for those desiring to explore the West as it was in the days of the pioneers and other groups traveling before modern vehicles were invented.

## Baggage trains

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wagon_train&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Baggage trains")]

The advent of gunpowder warfare meant that an army could no longer rely solely on foraging in the surrounding countryside, and required a regular supply of munitions.[[6]](#cite_note-Logistics_of_Liberty-6) In the 18th century, organized [commissary](/wiki/Commissary "Commissary") and [quartermaster](/wiki/Quartermaster "Quartermaster") departments were developed to centralize delivery of supplies.[[6]](#cite_note-Logistics_of_Liberty-6) The delivery took the form of "baggage trains", large groups of wagons that traveled at the rear of the main army.

## In popular media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Wagon_train&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In popular media")]

Westward-bound collective treks are reflected in numerous books, films, and television programs about the journeys. Examples include: [Emerson Hough](/wiki/Emerson_Hough "Emerson Hough")'s 1922 novel and [James Cruze](/wiki/James_Cruze "James Cruze")'s silent film based on it, *[The Covered Wagon](/wiki/The_Covered_Wagon "The Covered Wagon")* (1923); [Raoul Walsh](/wiki/Raoul_Walsh "Raoul Walsh")'s film *[The Big Trail](/wiki/The_Big_Trail "The Big Trail")* (1930); [Robert North Bradbury](/wiki/Robert_North_Bradbury "Robert North Bradbury")'s film *[Westward Ho](/wiki/Westward_Ho_(1935_film) "Westward Ho (1935 film)")* (1935); [John Ford](/wiki/John_Ford "John Ford")'s *[Wagon Master](/wiki/Wagon_Master "Wagon Master")* (1950) and the television series it inspired, *[Wagon Train](/wiki/Wagon_Train "Wagon Train")* (1957–1965); [William A. Wellman](/wiki/William_A._Wellman "William A. Wellman")'s film, *[Westward the Women](/wiki/Westward_the_Women "Westward the Women")* (1951); [A. B. Guthrie Jr.](/wiki/A._B._Guthrie_Jr. "A. B. Guthrie Jr.")'s 1949 novel *[The Way West](/wiki/The_Way_West "The Way West")* and [Andrew V. McLaglen](/wiki/Andrew_V._McLaglen "Andrew V. McLaglen")'s [1967 film](/wiki/The_Way_West_(film) "The Way West (film)") based on it; and the "[Wagons West](/wiki/Noel_Gerson "Noel Gerson")" series of 24 novels written by [Noel Gerson](/wiki/Noel_Gerson "Noel Gerson") (under the pseudonym Dana Fuller Ross) between 1979 and 1989.
